LAW AND MOTION 302, INTERVENOR PAYMENTS IP PTY LTD.'S DEMURRER TO THE FIFTH CAUSE OF ACTION TO QUIET TITLE IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND, (SEE CCP 430.10(E). INTERVENOR MAY DEMUR TO THE COMPLAINT AND PLAINTIFF FAILS TO SHOW THAT THE DEMURRER IS UNTIMELY. (SEE MAGUIRE VS. CUNNINGHAM (1923) 64 CAL.APP. 536, 540-541; DEUTSCHMANN VS. SEARS ROEBUCK AND CO. (1982) 132 CAL.APP.3D 912,916.) PLAINTIFF DOES NOT ALLEGE SUFFICIENT FACTS SHOWING A BASIS FOR HIS CLAIM OF TITLE TO THE DOMAIN NAME. (SEE CCP 761.020(B). "IT HAS BEEN HELD CONSISTENTLY THAT THE OWNER OF AN EQUITABLE INTEREST CANNOT MAINTAIN AN ACTION TO QUIET TITLE AGAINST THE OWNER OF THE LEGAL TITLE." (STAFFORD VS. BALLINGER (1962) 199 CAL.APP.2D289, 294-295) PLAINTIFF DOES NOT ADDUCE FACTS SHOWING THAT THERE IS A REASONABLE POSSIBILITY THAT HE CAN CURE THE DEFECT. PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE A FORM OF ORDER AND TO COMPLY WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: STEPHEN MURPHY; CLERK: CYNTHIA HERBERT; REPORTER: CAROL KAREN CSR #8189 (302)

Off Calendar Sep-22-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Richard Vasquez of the law offices of Vasquez Benisek (925-297-9499) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorneys, Daniel Bidell and Andrew T. Oliver of the law offices of Amin Turocy & Watson LLP (650-618-6477) appear as counsels for Intervenor/moving party-in-intervention, Payments IP

Joseph C. Gratz, of the law offices of Durie Tangri (415-362-6666) appears as counsel for cross-defendant, Enom, Inc.

No other appearance is made in this matter

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

INTERVENOR PAYMENTS IP PTY LTD.'s Motion For Preliminary Injunction Restraining The Administrative Freeze On Funding.Com is GRANTED. Upon filing of a bond by Payments IP in the amount of $200,000 in favor of plaintiff Suraj Rajwani, defendant eNOM, Inc. is ordered to remove all barriers to Payments IP's access to Funding.com. Payments IP has shown that it has a likelihood of success on the merits of its claim for full access to Funding.com, including showing that it is a bona fide purchaser of that domain name without any notice of the conflicting claim by Mr. Rajwani. Payments IP has also shown that it will suffer substantial irreparable harm by its continued lack of access to the domain name. While Mr. Rajwani has standing to oppose this motion, he has failed to show either a likelihood of success on the merits of his claim for specific performance or that he will be irreparably harmed by the granting of this motion. The $200,000 bond is the court's best estimate of the amount of damages and fees and costs that Mr. Rajwani might incur in the event he is successful in showing that Payments IP is not entitled to access to the domain name. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Cross Defendant Suraj Rajwani'S Motion To Strike Cross Complaint (For Tentative Ruling Only)
Off Calendar Aug-16-2017 Off Calendar

MOTION TO STRIKE CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-16-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on CROSS DEFENDANT SURAJ RAJWANI's MOTION TO STRIKE CROSS COMPLAINT as follows:
Plaintiff or cross-defendant Rajwani's anti-SLAPP motion to strike the cross complaint of Intervenor Payments IP Pty Ltd. is denied. The court concludes that Mr. Rajwani fails to show that Intervenor's claims arising from petitioning activity within the meaning of CCP 425.16(e). Therefore, Mr. Rajwani fails to meet prong 1 of the anti-SLAPP statute. In City of Cotati v. Cashman (2002) 29 Cal.4th 69, the California Supreme Court explained: "[T]he statutory phrase 'cause of action...arising from' means simply that the defendant's act underlying the plaintiff's cause of action must itself have been an act in furtherance of the right of petition or free speech. [Citation.] In the anti-SLAPP context, the critical point is whether the plaintiff's cause of action was based on an act in furtherance of the defendant's right of petition or free speech. [Citations.] 'A defendant meets this burden by demonstrating that the act underlying the plaintiff's cause fits one of the categories spelled out in section 425.16, subdivision (e)....'"(Id. at p. 78.) "In deciding whether the 'arising from' requirement is met, a court considers 'the pleadings, and supporting and opposing affidavits stating the facts upon which the liability or defense is based.' " (Cotati, supra, 29 Cal.4th at p. 79.) In this case, the gravamen of Intervenor's claims is based on the cross-defendants' refusal to allow access to Intervenor's website and the reference to the stipulation and lawsuit is merely incidental to Intervenor's claims. (See Martinez v. Metabolife Intl, Inc. (2003) 113 Cal.App.4th 181, 188 ("a defendant in an ordinary private dispute cannot take advantage of the anti-SLAPP statute simply because the complaint contains some references to speech or petitioning activity by the defendant.") In Renewable Resources Coalition, Inc. v. Pebble Mines Corp. (2013) 218 Cal.App.4th 384, the defendants purchased confidential documents from plaintiff's former fundraiser, then used those documents to prosecute a complaint against plaintiff for alleged election law violations. The court reversed the grant of defendants' special motion to strike: "Rather than focusing on the gravamen of this action, which was that the Pebble defendants allegedly purchased the Coalition's confidential documents, the trial court focused on the injury to the Coalition, which was forced to defend itself in the Alaska Public Offices Commission proceeding. However, the gravamen of an action is the allegedly wrongful and injury-producing conduct, not the damage which flows from such conduct. Here, the gravamen of the Coalition's action is the allegation that the Pebble defendants wrongfully purchased its confidential documents. Said purchase was not an act by defendants in furtherance of their right of petition or free speech." In this case, the gravamen of Intervenor's action is the wrongful shutdown of its website. In addition, if the court were to address prong 2 of the anti-SLAPP statute, the declarations of Mr. Bedell and Mr. Koch show a probability of prevailing on the claims. Intervenor correctly cites Kimmel v. Goland (1990) 51 Cal.3d 202, 211. The litigation privilege of CC 47 applies to communicative acts and it does not apply to cross-defendants' noncommunicative conduct, i.e. shutting down the website. The court denies Intervenor's fee request because the motion was not frivolous within the meaning of CCP 425.16(c)(1). The Court signed the proposed order submit by the prevailing party via email. Judge: Suzanne R. Bolanos; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court ruled as follows: INTERVENOR PAYMENTS IP PTY LTD.'s DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT - Intervenor Payments IP Pty Ltd.'s demurrer to the fifth cause of action to quiet title is sustained without leave to amend, (See CCP 430.10(e). Intervenor may demur to the complaint and plaintiff fails to show that the demurrer is untimely. (See Maguire vs. Cunningham (1923) 64 Cal.App. 536, 540-541; Deutschmann vs. Sears Roebuck and Co. (1982) 132 Cal.App.3d 912,916.) Plaintiff does not allege sufficient facts showing a basis for his claim of title to the domain name. (See CCP 761.020(b). "It has been held consistently that the owner of an equitable interest cannot maintain an action to quiet title against the owner of the legal title." (Stafford vs. Ballinger (1962) 199 Cal.App.2d289, 294-295) Plaintiff does not adduce facts showing that there is a reasonable possibility that he can cure the defect. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Stephen Murphy; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Carol Karen CSR #8189
